question_structure.context: As shown in the figure, the two series resistors $R_{1}=12k\Omega$, $R_{2}{=}36k\Omega$, and the voltage across terminals $A$ and $B$ remains constant at 15V.
question_structure.sub_question_1: What are the voltages across $R_{1}$ and $R_{2}$?
question_structure.sub_question_2: If the internal resistance of the voltmeter $V$ is $12k\Omega$, what are the readings of the voltmeter when S is in contact with $C$ and $D$, respectively?

sub_question_1.step_1: According to the voltage characteristics of a series circuit, it is known that: $U{\propto}R$

sub_question_2.step_4: When S is in contact with $C$, the voltmeter is connected in parallel with $R_{1}$, measuring the voltage across the parallel branch. The equivalent parallel resistance is $R_{parallel}=\frac{R_{1}R_{V}}{R_{1}+R_{V}}=\frac{12\times12}{12+12}k\Omega=6k\Omega$.
sub_question_2.step_5:
The translation of the given text into English, keeping all LaTeX formulas and physical expressions unchanged, is as follows:
$U_{parallel}=\frac{R_{parallel}}{R_{parallel}+R_{2}}U=\frac{6}{6+36}\times15\mathrm{V}=\frac{15}{7}\mathrm{V}$
sub_question_2.step_6: When S is in contact with $D$, $R_{parallel}^{\prime}=\frac{R_{2}R_{V}}{R_{2}+R_{V}}=\frac{36\times12}{36+12}k\Omega=9k\Omega$.

image_captions: The image depicts a simple electrical circuit diagram. Two resistors, labeled R1 and R2, are connected in series between points A and B. A voltmeter is connected between the junction of R1 and R2 and a switch, S. The switch can be in one of two states, C or D, connecting either of them to the junction of R1 and R2. The voltage source, labeled U, is connected between A and B, supplying power to the circuit.

step_1.physical_theorem: Voltage characteristics of a series circuit

result_quantity.name: Equivalent resistance of R1 and voltmeter
Voltage across the parallel branch when S is connected to C
Equivalent resistance of R2 and voltmeter
Voltage across the parallel branch when S is connected to D
